summary: Joel shares a usability anecdote from Mark Hurst's talk at the Reboot conference in Denmark. When checkout screens prompt for a username and password, some novice users type their AOL credentials, because they don't read the specific instructions and assume the simplest mental model — that one password works everywhere. Joel connects this to his broader principles that users don't read on-screen text and that they assume the simplest possible program model until proven wrong.
key_points: Users don't read instructional text on screens, even when it's directly relevant to what they need to do
People default to the simplest mental model — assuming their AOL password works on every site — until experience teaches them otherwise
Usability failures often stem from designers assuming users will read and process on-screen instructions
money_quotes: When your checkout screen prompts for a username and password, some newbie users will type in their AOL username and password.
More proof that people don't read.
People will assume the simplest possible program model — i.e., that their new AOL password works everywhere — until they learn otherwise.
key_insight: Users don't read instructions and will always assume the simplest possible mental model for how software works, which designers must account for.
